design_philosophy.core_loop: Recruit defeated generals, build a party of unique characters, engage in tactical turn-based combat to unify China under the Shu Han banner
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (recruitment bribes, party slots) versus character abundance (150 recruitables, but limited usefulness); managing a roster where most characters plateau in effectiveness
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Embodying Liu Bei as a military strategist uniting China during the Three Kingdoms period; assembling a legendary army of historical generals through persuasion and conquest
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Radical departure from standard RPG design: replaces generic random encounters with strategic recruitment encounters against named generals. Introduces 'All-Out' auto-battle to reduce tedium in trivial fights, trusting player judgment on when to autopilot. Deliberately creates character bloat as a design consequence—150 recruitable characters but only ~5-7 remain viable late-game, forcing meaningful roster decisions.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Significant control through recruitment mechanics—players can grind for stronger generals, accumulate wealth/horses for bribes, and selectively eject weak party members to recruit stronger alternatives. Auto-battle ('All-Out') option allows players to skip tedious low-threat encounters while maintaining tactical control over dangerous fights. Branching story paths provide exploration-based difficulty variance.

progression.upgrade_system: Character recruitment system replacing traditional leveling; generals join party after defeat with random probability (bribe required). Experience accumulation is present but many characters cap their growth early, creating soft obsolescence. Active party of 7 (5 combat + 1 substitute + 1 tactician/reinforcement) creates meaningful composition decisions.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Narrative progression through defeating generals and regional conquest; branching story paths unlock alternate routes through Three Kingdoms conflict. Party slot management unlocks new recruitment opportunities.
replayability_hooks: 150 unique generals with different recruitment timings create varied party compositions across playthroughs
Branching storyline paths alter tactical and narrative experience
Resource scarcity (money, horses, party slots) creates different recruitment priorities per run
Tactical system depth rewards optimizing team synergy rather than stat-chasing

world_building_techniques: Loose adaptation of Romance of the Three Kingdoms historical novel, grounding narrative in recognizable historical period
Recruited generals serve as environmental storytelling—their presence on the map indicates territorial control and faction strength
Branching narrative paths reflect player agency in historical outcome ('altered in Liu Bei's favor' against Cao Wei and Sun Wu in late-game deviations)
Character recruitment dialogue implicitly reveals motivations for joining Liu Bei's cause
Generational narrative structure mirroring actual Three Kingdoms conflict progression

boss_design.philosophy: Named generals function as both boss encounters and recruitment targets, collapsing the boss/unit distinction. Victory leads to potential recruitment (random chance) rather than permanent elimination, creating strategic value in sparing enemies. This transforms bosses from purely climactic encounters into permanent roster acquisitions.
notable_bosses: Zhang Jiao (sorcerer commanding Yellow Turban rebels, opening conflict)
Cao Wei faction warlords (late-game adversaries with altered outcomes favoring player)
Sun Wu kingdom forces (opposing unification attempts)

faction_indicators: Recruitment success rate signals allegiance—defeated generals joining indicate faction loyalty shifts
Party composition reflects political coalition (historical generals grouped by historical faction)
Regional territorial control indicated by which generals roam particular lands
danger_signals: Encounters with unrecruited high-tier generals in new territories signal faction resistance escalation
Repeated encounters with same generals indicate failed recruitment attempts and hardening opposition
Resource depletion (insufficient bribes/horses) signals weakening diplomatic capacity
environmental_storytelling: Roaming generals on maps establish faction presence without cutscenes
Map regions populated with specific generals reflect territorial control and historical period progression
Transition from Yellow Turban rebels to established warlord armies tracks Three Kingdoms formation
Character skill availability tied to recruitment success reveals power accumulation narratively
